title: NOC 21203 – Land Surveyors | GTR Immigration
description: Land Surveyors play a crucial role in infrastructure development, property transactions, Indigenous land claims, and natural-resource projects across Canada. Classified under NOC 21203 (TEER 1), this profession qualifies for skilled-immigration streams such as Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs), employer-supported work permits, and long-term Permanent Residence (PR) strategies.

classification.category: 2 – Natural and applied sciences and related occupations
classification.teer: 1 – Occupations usually require a university degree
classification.major_group: 212 – Professional occupations in applied sciences (except engineering)
classification.minor_group: 2120 – Architects, urban planners, and land surveyors

duties: Develop survey plans and legal-survey procedures
Establish and mark property and land boundaries
Determine locations using GPS, drones, and EDM tools
Analyze data using GIS and CAD systems
Prepare official survey plans and reports
Certify property boundaries and assume legal responsibility
Testify as expert witnesses when required
employment_requirements: Bachelor’s degree in geomatics or survey engineering
Or college diploma + academic credits + regional board exams
job_outlook: Between 2022–2031, Canada is projected to create 3,500 new Land-Surveyor positions, with around 2,900 job seekers—indicating a favourable immigration outlook, particularly for internationally trained professionals who secure provincial licensing and employer sponsorship.
additional_information: Land Surveyors remain in strong demand nationwide due to:
Housing-development expansionInfrastructure and transportation projectsMining and energy explorationIndigenous land-claim settlementsUrban-growth planning
Federal, provincial, and municipal governmentsPrivate surveying firmsEngineering and construction companiesReal-estate developersNatural-resource companiesSelf-employed practices
Annual earnings usually range between $45,100–$96,300 CAD, depending on licensing, province, and experience.
Why Choose Canada as a Land Surveyor?

High demand across multiple provincesMandatory licensing that supports stable wagesPR-linked skilled-worker pathwaysProvincial nomination opportunitiesUniversal healthcareFamily sponsorship optionsMulticultural professional communities
